(GAIA) secure, government-wide platform that enables federal entities to rapidly develop, deploy, and operate AI agents. It automates internal workflows, augments employee tasks, and powers AI-driven public service delivery at scale , providing a shared sovereign infrastructure layer so individual ministries do not need to build foundational AI capability from scratch.

“Procedures”

1. Addressing the Lack of Policies The Ministry of Post and Telecommunications (MPTC) has developped the (draft) National AI Strategy for Cambodia. Currently in the final stages of endorsement, this framework is designed to institutionalize AI governance. It focuses on solidifying existing digital initiatives while creating a cohesive roadmap that integrates all components of the AI ecosystem under a unified regulatory umbrella.

2. Addressing the Lack of Data The draft strategy explicitly prioritizes data availability and readiness. It outlines a mandate to encourage both public and private stakeholders to begin the systematic collection, digitization, and processing of datasets. By establishing standards for data interoperability, the initiative aims to build a robust foundation for future AI use-cases and evidence-based decision-making. All of these elements are reflected within our (draft) National AI Strategy.

1818 MIA Chatbot - hybrid chatbot with text-to-speech and speech-to-text functionalities to support clients of various connected public services. The MIA chatbot is an in‑house, custom‑developed hybrid chatbot service that we have been using at the 1818 Government Hotline since 2022 not to replace human intelligence, but to support and enhance operators' work. In 2025, MIA was already able to respond to 63% of customer inquiries without operator intervention. However, in more complex life situations - where precise answers are required and hallucination‑related errors must be avoided - the chatbot redirects the conversation to a human operator. These cases fall into two categories: in most of them, the chatbot still provides answer suggestions to the operator, who can select from them, significantly speeding up response times. In the more complicated cases making up a few percent of all questions, the operator provides the final answer directly.
Currently, MIA does not use a large language model; therefore, it does not perform LLM‑based analysis of customer questions and does not search in any RAG-model knowledge base. Instead, it learns exclusively from conversations between operators and customers, and only provides automated responses for pre‑validated question‑answer pairs.

Artificial Intelligence Virtual Assistant (AIVA): Automates pay intake, calculations, and document handling, with all outputs reviewed, validated, and approved by compensation professionals.

The Compensation Virtual Assistant chatbot provides timely, reliable answers drawn from over 80,000 business rules, 150+ collective agreements, and more than 40,000 pages of directives, terms and conditions, and salary tables.

AIVA is a key component of the Government of Canada’s compensation services modernization. It supports the transformation of the HR-to-Pay intake process from a manual, transaction-based model to an efficient, data-driven, and client-centric approach. By combining intelligent automation with a user-friendly interface, AIVA reduces administrative burden, accelerates intake processing, and enables Compensation Advisors to focus on higher-value work.

Digital Eye – Liver Project: The project performs AI-powered mass detection and classification. The AI models developed were integrated into the reporting system of the Ministry of Health’s Cancer Early Diagnosis Screening and Education Center, and the system’s first version was put into use. This provided support to relevant specialists for the faster and prioritized reporting of patients with potentially malignant masses. Additionally, an AI-based decision support system was provided to radiologists for testing purposes during the review of patients’ mammography images at hospitals affiliated with Başkent and Ankara Universities.

Digital Eye – Mammography Project: aims to revolutionize the early diagnosis of breast cancer. The goal is to enhance the accuracy of radiologists’ mammography evaluation processes and minimize issues arising from human error through a newly developed AI-powered decision support system. The AI model analyzes mammography images to identify high-risk cases and prioritizes these for review by radiologists.

Hungary’s Artificial Intelligence Strategy (2025–2030), formulated by the AI Coalition alongside the Government, as well as academic, and business stakeholders, aims to establish the nation as a regional leader in AI development. The strategy serves as a flexible framework, updated annually to adapt to rapid technological progress. Its primary objective is to maintain global competitiveness while ensuring that AI remains human-centric, transparent, and socially beneficial.
The strategy is organised into three core pillars: "AI for Society", "AI for Technology", and "AI for Business". The social pillar focuses on making AI benefits accessible to all citizens through Hungarian Large Language Models (LLMs) in public services and AI-driven diagnostics in healthcare. In education, personalised digital assistants will support students, while smart solutions will be prioritised for transport and energy.
To strengthen the economy, the government provides funding and innovation centres to assist small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in adopting AI. This initiative spans sectors such as manufacturing, agriculture, and finance to drive GDP growth and job creation. Additionally, the technological pillar fosters research into open-source models and autonomous systems, ultimately striving for digital sovereignty and an enhanced quality of life.

Key action 1 – AI Literacy 
Building AI literacy across government enables public officers to make informed decisions about AI adoption whilst understanding inherent risks and limitations. Through comprehensive training and resources (including a mandatory e-learning curriculum and quiz), officers develop the competencies to evaluate AI solutions critically, ensuring they can harness AI's potential whilst maintaining ethical standards and public trust. This foundational knowledge empowers users to implement AI responsibly, from initial development through to deployment and ongoing management.

Key action 2 – Domain Transformation 
We work with agencies and domain experts to identify high-impact use cases that have the potential to deliver genuine value to citizens and government operations. Rather than focusing on incremental productivity improvements, this approach reimagines business processes and workflows. This requires starting with clear, high-impact problems, redesigning processes around them, and integrating AI where it measurably improves outcomes.

Key action 3 – AI Governance 
Our AI governance approach enables innovation and promotes responsible use. The AI Governance framework prescribed across the Singapore Government emphasises agencies' responsibility for their own AI use cases whilst maintaining central oversight. The framework is designed to be adaptive, with regular policy reviews that reflect new technology developments and evolving government risk posture, ensuring governance remains relevant and effective. Beyond establishing frameworks, agencies receive practical support through detailed guidelines and technical tools that facilitate policy compliance.

Key Initiatives Addressing Barriers to AI Adoption in Egypt (2023–2026)
1. National AI Governance and Strategy
•	Egypt National AI Strategy 2025–2030 led by the National Council for Artificial Intelligence, Quantum Computing and Emerging Technologies provides a national framework to scale AI adoption across sectors while addressing governance, ethics, and infrastructure gaps.
•	Establishment of the Responsible AI Center to operationalize the Egyptian Charter for Responsible AI and develop frameworks, tools, and best practices for trustworthy AI deployment (ECRAI).
•	Issuing the Guide and Guidelines for  responsible AI by ECRAI 
•	Issuing the Guidelines on GENAI 
2. Data Governance and Regulatory Framework
•	Issuance of Executive Regulations for the Personal Data Protection Law (Law 151/2020) in 2025, clarifying compliance obligations such as data processing permits, security measures, breach notification, and cross-border transfer rules.
•	Adoption of Egypt’s Open Data Policy to improve government data availability and support AI innovation while preparing for a comprehensive Data Governance Law.
3. Digital Infrastructure and Cloud Enablement
•	Adoption of the Cloud First Policy by the Supreme Council of Digital Society to prioritize cloud-based solutions in government digital transformation and support scalable AI and data analytics infrastructure.
•	Expansion of digital infrastructure and high-performance computing capabilities through the Applied Innovation Center to support national AI projects.
4. Skills Development and Human Capital
•	Digital Egypt initiatives (Cubs, Marvels, Pioneers, and Builders) aimed at developing digital and AI skills for school students, university students, and graduates.
•	AI Capacity Building Initiative providing training for university professors and students and enabling integration of AI curricula into universities.
•	ITIDA and SECC programs delivering AI and generative AI training for ICT professionals, developers, and enterprises.
5. Innovation, Startups, and Ecosystem Support
•	TIEC incubation and accelerator programs (Start IT, Entrepreneurship Accelerator, Innovation Clusters) providing mentoring, funding, and technical support for AI startups.
•	Applied Innovation Center developing applied AI solutions for sectors such as healthcare, agriculture, and Arabic natural language processing.
6. Inclusion and Women’s Empowerment
•	Qodwa-Tech initiative empowering women entrepreneurs through digital and AI skills training, mentorship, and peer-learning networks.
•	Programs supporting women’s participation in digital entrepreneurship and AI-enabled e-commerce.
7. International Cooperation and Standards
•	Participation in OECD AI initiatives and UNESCO AI Readiness Assessment (RAM) to align Egypt’s AI governance with international best practices.
•	Stakeholder consultations and capacity-building programs to integrate global standards into national AI policy.
•	Egypt applying for membership to the Global Partnership for AI
•	Egypt is friend of the HAIP

La Agencia de Gobierno Electrónico y Sociedad de la Información y del Conocimiento (Agesic) lidera la elaboración de la Hoja de Ruta 2026-2027 para la implementación de las Estrategias Nacionales de Inteligencia Artificial (IA) y de Datos, en coordinación con el Comité Estratégico de IA y Datos para el Sector Público.
En dicho documento se han incorporado iniciativas alineadas con las Líneas de Acción definidas en las estrategias mencionadas, entre las cuales se destacan:
- Consolidación del ecosistema institucional: desarrollo de un modelo de gobernanza y mecanismos de coordinación multiactor que fortalezcan la institucionalidad en materia de IA y datos.
- Marco normativo habilitante: propuesta orientada a potenciar el desarrollo tecnológico y la innovación, mitigando riesgos y garantizando la protección de derechos individuales y colectivos. En este sentido, se toma como antecedente el Informe artículo 74 de la Ley N.º 20.212 (“Recomendaciones para una regulación de la inteligencia artificial orientada al desarrollo ético, la protección de los derechos humanos y el fomento de la innovación tecnológica”), presentado por Agesic, que busca generar un enfoque país sobre la regulación de IA y datos, promoviendo su adopción responsable y segura.
- Compras públicas y ciberseguridad: incorporación de cláusulas modelo en los procesos de adquisiciones vinculadas a la gestión de datos y soluciones tecnológicas, así como la adaptación del Marco de Ciberseguridad de Uruguay a los desafíos que plantea la IA.
- Gestión del talento y futuro del trabajo: impulso de programas de formación y capacitación en IA y Ciencia de Datos tanto en el ámbito de la educación formal —desde la educación inicial— como en el informal. Se incluyen iniciativas dirigidas al funcionariado público y al público en general, además de un programa para consolidar una comunidad de práctica en datos con enfoque multiactor.
- Gestión integral de datos en el Estado: establecimiento de un modelo operativo para la gestión ética y eficiente de los datos en los organismos públicos, asegurando estándares de calidad y responsabilidad en su utilización

México está trabajando de manera coordinada en la Ley y la Estrategia Nacional de IA. A este proceso se han sumado varios actores, haciendo de este ejercicio un ejercicio multiactor. 
Respecto a la protección de datos, México cuenta con la Ley General de Protección de Datos Personales en Posesión de Sujetos Obligados (LGPDPPSO), publicada en el DOF el 20 de marzo de 2025, que actualiza el régimen de protección de datos en el sector público. 

Además, México ya cuenta con una Política General de Ciberseguridad para la Administración Pública Federal, publicada en el DOF el 17 de diciembre de 2025 y emitida por la ATDT. Crea un marco común de ciberseguridad para toda la APF con el objetivo de salvaguardar los derechos digitales, proteger la información gubernamental y garantizar la continuidad de los servicios públicos esenciales. La ATDT será la autoridad rectora, a través de la Dirección General de Ciberseguridad, encargándose de emitir disposiciones normativas, coordinar la gestión de incidentes y liderar estrategias de capacitación.

National AI Academy:  Establishment of a comprehensive National AI Academy that serves government, the private sector, academia, and civil society alike. Such an Academy would institutionalize AI literacy, ethics, governance, and technical capacity-building across all sectors, ensuring that leaders, policymakers, developers, and users have the knowledge and skills to design, deploy, and oversee AI systems responsibly. It would integrate training on ethical standards, risk management, data governance, transparency, accountability, and human oversight into structured certification programs and continuous learning pathways, while also acting as a central hub for research, innovation, and cross-sector collaboration. By embedding responsible AI principles into national skills development and professional standards, the AI Academy would create a sustainable, ecosystem-wide foundation for trustworthy, inclusive, and human-centered AI adoption.

The AI Strategy for the Federal Public Service 2025-2027 has four priorities:
• Create central capacity to support departments with AI adoption through an AI centre of expertise and access to common infrastructure.
• Ensure fit-for-purpose AI policy and governance by establishing common governance and risk management frameworks, addressing policy and legislative alignment, gaps and barriers, and embedding a requirement to “think AI” in planning and funding processes.
• Ensure the public service has the necessary AI talent through skill benchmarking and training, recruitment, and retention planning.
• Strengthening transparency and public trust through an AI register, plain language notification of AI use, clear metrics of impact and adoption, and ongoing engagement with partners, stakeholders and the public.

The Austrian Government views the use of AI in government operations as a critical lever for efficiency. Our approach is characterized by a “human-in-the-loop” and “human-in-control” philosophy, ensuring that AI supports, rather than replaces, the civil servants in their daily tasks. The Austrian Government aims to address certain challenges with the strengthening of the use of AI, most prominently the demographic shift within the public service workforce.
For this, the Federal Chancellery has initiated the “Public AI” initiative that aims to support the development of so-called Shared Services. With the initiative the publicly owned IT-service provider “Bundesrechenzentrum” will provide a fully sovereign AI infrastructure platform (Large-Language-Model-as-a-Service) for all federal ministries. 
Currently, the Federal Chancellery is planning the rollout of specific internal use cases, supporting the daily work of public servants, like a general LLM system and RAG-based system with specific knowledge sources. The Electronic Data Management system (ELAK) will also be upgraded with AI capabilities, supporting the faster processing of files and folders by the public servants.

Austria has established a “Digital Competencies Initiative” (Digitale Kompetenzoffensive) as an intersectional cooperation between 4 federal ministries. The initiative focuses on the development and coordination of measures for digital literacy and skills. Under the umbrella of the initiative, the Digital Competencies and Skills strategy outlines how Austria is going to achieve more digital competencies among the citizens. Measures around AI are a major priority in the strategy.
Accordingly, a specific strategy for AI literacy and skills is currently in development, the “AI Skills Austria” plan or the “Action Plan on AI Skills for Austria”. Following the explicit goals and principles of the general national digital literacy and skills strategy and the national AI strategy, the AI literacy and skills strategy will focus on the inclusion of population groups with low levels of digital literacy like the elderly and rural population and gender diversity for AI skills with the aim to broaden the national IT experts pool. The plan establishes an AI competency stack, consisting of three levels of expertise (AI experts, AI application skills, Basic AI skills) and complemented by concrete flagship initiatives.
The School of Data Public Services is a programme at the Austrian Federal Academy of Public Administration that was launched in 2017 with the aim of training federal employees on various aspects of working with data. It provides the necessary digital skills to meet the current challenges of data management, analysis and visualisation in the public sector. The aim is to train employees and managers in handling data in order to increase the efficiency and transparency of administration and enable data-driven decision-making. The programme covers the areas of data & administration, technology & tools, analysis & visualisation and management & organisation.
The Government has adopted AI Policy. We are in the process of drafting AI Guidelines

The government has set up two joint committees to develop the digital vision for 2030 (DRC Digital Nation 2030) and the national AI strategy, whose work will be completed by the end of March 2026. In the meantime, the government has adopted a national data governance strategy that addresses the issue of data quality.

The government is investing in the development of computing infrastructure for AI needs. Additional servers are being purchased and deployed in secure data centers on a phased basis. This makes it possible to deploy AI models and process sensitive government data within the country. An automated information system for exchanging data from government databases for the provision of public services has also been created. A government data interoperability platform has also been developed at the pilot stage. These efforts should ensure the widespread use of AI in the public sector within a few years.

The main initiative is the publication of the National Strategy for AI (ANIA), which establishes a clear vision for the ethical, safe, and responsible use of AI, bringing Portugal closer to the forefront of European competitiveness. It focuses on transforming innovation into concrete public value, namely higher wages, more efficient public services, and a higher quality of life. ANIA is governed by six guiding principles and is structured around four areas of action with clear objectives.

Firstly, Infrastructure and Data, to ensure that Portugal develops strategic computing capacity and a robust data economy, reducing external dependence, guaranteeing diplomatic coordination to protect continuous access to critical AI resources, and creating technological foundations that allow it to compete in the next decade.

Secondly, Innovation and Adoption, to protect fundamental AI research and accelerate AI adoption across the economy, particularly SMEs, ensuring that the Public Administration is a pioneer in its adoption, promoting strong collaboration and integration within the ecosystem with a strong focus on product. Third, Talent and Skills, to ensure that Portugal trains, attracts, mobilizes, and retains AI talent on the scale necessary to sustain the country's competitiveness, strengthening the capacity of the training system, limiting the brain drain of qualified talent, and ensuring that both the private and public sectors have accelerated plans for skills development for the sustainability of work in the AI age.
Fourth, Responsibility and Ethics, to promote a responsible and sustainable AI research and development ecosystem, ensuring an effective and efficient regulatory regime that protects citizens and enables business innovation in a safe, transparent, ethical manner, aligned with European standards.

ANIA is operationalized through 32 initiatives, involving public policies throughout the entire ecosystem among universities, research centers, companies (including startups), and Public Administration, ensuring an integrated approach that covers the entire innovation value cycle and technology maturity. ANIA is the main instrument for aligning Portugal with the AI
Continent Action Plan and the Apply AI Strategy, positioning the country to contribute to European goals and objectives in the field of AI. At the same time, ANIA guides the country to strategically take advantage of key European funding opportunities, which are fundamental to accelerating digital transformation and strengthening national competitiveness.

ANIA aims to make Portugal one of the most advanced European countries in the application of AI for productivity and the public good, ensuring that technology contributes to a more productive, innovative and competitive economy and to a more efficient, transparent and people-centered State. ANIA is therefore a strategic instrument for transforming technological potential into economic and social value, ensuring a responsible transition oriented towards the public interest.

The Singapore Government addresses key challenges in responsible and effective AI deployment through a comprehensive, multi-layered strategy that tackles fundamental infrastructure and governance issues. 

High-quality data is important for AI systems to function effectively. As AI use grows in the public sector, agencies will need to evolve their data architecture and engineering capabilities to support more complex and scalable AI workloads. The Singapore Government’s Government Data Architecture (GDA) was set up to lay the foundation for the shared infrastructure to share data safely and efficiently. Single Sources of Truth (SSOTs) are designated to provide clean, verified and authoritative core data fields commonly used for policy making and service delivery. Trusted Centre (TCs) are responsible for fusing data from SSOTs and sharing it across whole-of-Government. The Public Sector Governance Act (PSGA) provides a clear legal foundation for data sharing across government agencies.Complementing the PSGA, comprehensive set of rules and principles for data governance are established in the Instruction Manual for Infocomm Technology and Smart Systems Management (IM8) establishing clear responsibilities and accountability of both data providing and data consuming parties, and ensure that data is safeguarded according to its sensitivity and risk profile. To facilitate better whole of government data discovery and data sharing, a central platform was developed to serve as the technical enabler for seamless data sharing across government, facilitating access to quality datasets whilst implementing standardised metadata and documentation practices that are crucial for AI model development and validation. 

AI also requires modern, scalable, interoperable infrastructure that connects to existing systems and databases. To overcome legacy interoperability constraints, we are modernising our systems and infrastructure to build scalable, interoperable foundations for AI—shifting to modular, cloud-based architectures and ensuring connectivity to existing systems and databases so AI can integrate with operations rather than remain as isolated pilots. We are putting data quality at the core of this effort by planning systematic collection, cleaning, standardisation, integration and validation for each use case, and evolving our data architecture and engineering capabilities so shared assets, smooth data flows, system interoperability and strong lineage/auditability become the default. We do this by providing agencies with technical resources via the Government Technology Agency, and by raising capabilities through Data and Modernisation Playbooks. We are also creating an enabling environment with secure, cloud-ready devices and access to central AI tools so officers can use modern capabilities in day-to-day work. Without these foundations agencies struggle to scale AI effectively beyond proofs of concept.

Our Government Technology Agency (GovTech) supports agencies to build and scale their systems and solutions, and empowers agencies through comprehensive education, advanced tools, and strategic capability development. GovTech has deployed about 1,700 officers across 56 agencies, including Chief Information Officers (CIOs), Chief Information Security Officers (CISOs) and IT delivery teams. This ensures that agencies benefit from technical skills and expertise while retaining the prerogative to discharge their accountabilities and make policy-operational decisions, even as they meet governance standards.

To address concerns about whether AI can meet our needs, public officers and leaders must first develop sufficient understanding of AI. This is why we have introduced a mandatory AI Literacy course for all public officers, helping them build foundational knowledge of AI, including its limitations and risks. The curriculum will be regularly updated to keep pace with technological developments and enhance our officers' capabilities. Additionally, public service leaders (from Permanent Secretaries, Chief Executives, Deputy Secretaries, to other senior deputies and directors) receive training on topics such as modernising systems, building better products, cybersecurity, data management, and AI adoption and governance. This equips decision-makers with the essential insights needed for responsible AI adoption. 

Public officers and leaders need assurance on safety, reliability, and fitness for purpose of AI solutions before scaling adoption. We have instituted policies that require all AI use cases to evaluate accuracy, safety and performance before deployment, and developed AI safety tools such as Litmus (baseline safety/security tests) and Sentinel (safety guardrails) to implement policy requirements. Product teams are encouraged to validate their problem statement and real-world utility through targeted user testing and pilots. These are backed by capability building efforts, such as a mandatory AI literacy module for public officers to help officers understand the benefits and limitations of AI, and practitioner communities such as Lorong AI, for AI practitioners to collaborate and share best practices. Taken together, this approach builds confidence that AI solutions are safe, effective, and responsive to operational needs before broad rollout.

The UAE addresses AI-related barriers through an integrated programme of governance, capability-building, and infrastructure reforms:
•       Governance and ethics The UAE Charter for the Development and Use of AI (2024), AI Ethics Guide (2022), and International Policy on AI (2024) provide a comprehensive principles-and-practice framework, complemented by Federal Decree-Law No. 45 of 2021 on Personal Data Protection.
•       Institutional capacity  Appointment of Chief AI Officers (CAIOs) in every federal and Dubai government entity, the AI, Digital Economy and Remote Work Applications Office, and the Artificial Intelligence and Advanced Technology Council (AIATC) under Law No. 3 of 2024.
•       Talent Mohamed bin Zayed University of AI (MBZUAI), the UAE National AI Programme in partnership with Oxford, the UAE Government Leaders Programme, and golden-visa pathways for senior AI experts.
•       Sovereign AI capability  Falcon LLM (Technology Innovation Institute), the GAIA federal AI platform, and the MGX investment vehicle supporting frontier compute, data centres, and AI infrastructure.
•       Data and interoperability UAE PASS digital identity, the federal data exchange, the once-only principle, and the Bayanat geospatial platform reduce legacy system friction for AI projects.
•       Safe experimentation  Regulatory sandboxes at DIFC, ADGM, and federal level enable controlled testing of AI applications before scaling.

Title: Breast Cancer (MASA)
Application Domain Healthcare
	 
Technology Domain Computer vision
Brief:
Early detection is the most powerful tool in the fight against breast cancer. The Breast Cancer (MASA) AI-driven diagnostic solution supports radiologists and oncologists, enhancing the accuracy of mammogram interpretations, ensuring that life-saving diagnoses happen with an accuracy close to 90% for Egyptian women.
Description: 
Breast cancer is the most common cancer occurring in women worldwide. Early diagnosis of breast cancer can lead to proper treatment of affected patients, increase breast cancer survival rates, and significant reduction of treatment costs, personal trauma, and social burden. Screening for early diagnosis and treatment is a major step towards fighting the disease. Using artificial intelligence for screening and early detection can help with the process.
The Breast Cancer (MASA) solution has been developed using artificial intelligence, specifically computer vision techniques, to assist radiologists and oncologists in breast cancer detection during mammogram-based screening. The center in partnership with the Baheya Foundation created a reliable, and high-quality data set that was be used for training the AI models using mammograms from 60K Egyptian women.
The solution analyzes mammogram images to detect abnormal masses, classify them as benign or malignant, identify calcifications, detect asymmetrical features between breasts, while considering the breast tissue density according to the ACR classification. Results are presented according to the global BI-RADS standard. The solution accuracy is over 90% for Egyptian women.
With the deployment of the solution in Baheya hospitals, and solution launch in August 2025, the solution will help automate some of the breast cancer diagnosis tasks, aiding medical staff, reducing the examination costs, increasing the efficiency of this process, and making it available to a larger number of women for periodic checkup.

TM Checker is IP Australia's free trade mark availability check designed to lower the bar of entry for small to medium businesses by simplifying the learning process of a complex IP system. The tool allows customers to check the potential registrability of their trademarks and then proceed through the application process. TM Checker guides customers through a step-by-step process. It leverages AI-powered search tools to identify potential issues. This includes similarity and distinctiveness, the most common reasons for rejection.

Technical Cooperation Project with JICA:
The Ministry of Digital Economy and Entrepreneurship is working to implement a joint technical cooperation with the Japan International Cooperation Agency (JICA). The project aims to build and develop an ecosystem supporting artificial intelligence in Jordan. This project aims to:
•	Strengthen and build an ecosystem supporting artificial intelligence in Jordan.
•	Implement AI use case models in vital, priority economic sectors.
•	Benefit from Japan's expertise in this field, as part of the cooperation between the Jordanian and Japanese governments.
•	Train leaders and some public sector employees by learning about Japanese AI practices.


Cooperation with the European Union through Twinning Program
•	The Twinning Project in the Field of Artificial Intelligence and Personal Data Protection. The Ministry of Digital Economy and Entrepreneurship is working on the Twinning Program in coordination and cooperation with the Ministry of Planning and the European Union (EU) to provide a resident twinning advisor and a group of short-term experts from EU countries, led by Greece, Spain, and Germany.
•	Contributing to Jordan’s economic development and citizen well-being by supporting digital transition through harnessing Artificial Intelligence (AI) technologies and Personal Data Protection (PDP) measures according to the EU best practices. 

Cooperation with the Economic and Social Commission for Western Asia (ESCWA)
•	Developing standards for classification the  Companies working in the field of AI : This initiative aims to establish clear frameworks for classifying companies operating in this sector, thus facilitating the provision of support and incentives.
•	Developing Guidelines for the Use of Generative AI: The Ministry is working with ESCWA to formulate guidelines that ensure the safe and ethical use of generative AI technologies.
•	Studying the Investment Environment for the AI Sector: This study aims to analyze the opportunities and challenges for attracting foreign and domestic investments, contributing to making Jordan a regional hub for digital innovation.
